title: Add a Suggested Self-Study Schedule for Beginners
author: sanskargujjar0001-tech
body:
I would like to suggest adding a recommended self-study schedule to help beginners follow the course more easily.

The course content is well organized into weeks, but many self-learners are unsure how to plan their daily or weekly study routine. A suggested roadmap would make it easier to stay consistent and complete the course.

Add an optional "Suggested Self-Study Schedule" section in the README that includes:
Recommended lessons for each week
Suggested daily study time (for example, 1–2 hours)
Project milestones
A simple progress checklist
This would be optional guidance and would not change the existing course structure.
Thank you for considering this suggestion!

title: Sparse checkout instructions exclude lesson assets needed for hands-on projects[Docs + DX]
author: adjetey0
body:
Description
The README recommends using sparse checkout to avoid downloading 50+ translations:

git sparse-checkout set --no-cone '/*' '!translations' '!translated_images'

Problem: Several lessons reference images and starter files that live inside `translations/` even for the English course. Example: `1-getting-started-lessons/3-accessibility/translations/README.md` contains the main lesson for some languages, but `1-getting-started-lessons/3-accessibility/assignment.md` links to `./images/terrarium.png` which only exists in `translated_images/espanol/images/`.

Result: Students following the sparse checkout steps get broken images and missing starter files for lessons like the terrarium, space game, and browser extension. They think the repo is broken.

Proposed Solution
1. *Audit lesson dependencies*: Move all required assets for the default English track out of `translations/` and `translated_images/` into each lesson folder.
2. *Update sparse checkout command*: If some shared assets must stay in `translated_images`, change the command to:

   git sparse-checkout set --no-cone '/*' '!translations' 'translated_images/english'

   Or keep a `/assets` folder at root with all required images.
3. *Add warning*: Note in README that sparse checkout will break specific lessons unless assets are included.
4. *CI check*: Add GitHub Action to fail if any `_.md` in default lessons links to a path excluded by the recommended sparse checkout.

Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Cloning with README sparse checkout command lets users complete all 24 lessons without 404s for images/assets
- [ ] No lesson `.md` in the English path references `translations/` or `translated_images/`
- [ ] README clearly lists which lessons break if users exclude images
- [ ] Test: Run sparse checkout, then `find . -name "_.html" -o -name "*.md" | xargs grep "src=" | grep "404"` returns nothing

Technical Notes
Affected lessons so far: `terrarium` project, `typing-game`, `browser-extension`. All link to images under `translated_images/`. The course is “project-based” so broken assets block completion.

Priority:High — breaks onboarding for beginners, which is the core audience
*Labels:* `bug`, `documentation`, `good-first-issue`, `help-wanted`

title: [agentic-workflows] Failed runs
author: github-actions[bot]
body:
This issue tracks all failures from agentic workflows in this repository. Each failed workflow run creates a sub-issue linked here for organization and easy filtering.

### Purpose

This parent issue helps you:
- View all workflow failures in one place by checking the sub-issues below
- Filter out failure issues from your main issue list using `no:parent-issue`
- Track the health of your agentic workflows over time

### Sub-Issues

All individual workflow failure issues are linked as sub-issues below. Click on any sub-issue to see details about a specific failure.

### Troubleshooting Failed Workflows

#### Using agentic-workflows Agent (Recommended)

**Agent:** `agentic-workflows`  
**Purpose:** Debug and fix workflow failures

**Instructions:**

1. Invoke the agent: Type `/agent` in GitHub Copilot Chat and select **agentic-workflows**
2. Provide context: Tell the agent to **debug** the workflow failure
3. Supply the workflow run URL for analysis
4. The agent will:
   - Analyze failure logs
   - Identify root causes
   - Propose specific fixes
   - Validate solutions

#### Using gh aw CLI

You can also debug failures using the `gh aw` CLI:

```bash
# Download and analyze workflow logs
gh aw logs <workflow-run-url>

# Audit a specific workflow run
gh aw audit <run-id>
```

#### Manual Investigation

1. Click on a sub-issue to see the failed workflow details
2. Follow the workflow run link in the issue
3. Review the agent job logs for error messages
4. Check the workflow configuration in your repository

### Resources

- [GitHub Agentic Workflows Documentation](https://github.com/github/gh-aw)
- [Troubleshooting Guide](https://github.com/github/gh-aw/blob/main/docs/troubleshooting.md)

title: Feature Request: Support for Student Certificates in Learning-Based Implementations
author: nfsprogramming
body:
Hello Microsoft Team,

I recently cloned this repository and built a frontend learning platform based on it. The goal of the platform is to help students easily understand technical topics through simple explanations and practical examples.

Since the platform is used for learning purposes, I would like to introduce a certificate of completion system for students who successfully finish learning modules or tutorials.

Feature Idea

It would be very useful if the project could support or provide guidance for:

A built-in certificate generation system

A way to track completed learning modules

Optional badges or achievement system for students

Documentation about educational usage of the repository

Use Case

Many students are using the platform to learn beginner technical topics. Providing certificates would help motivate them and allow them to showcase their learning progress.

Questions

Is it allowed to generate completion certificates for students using this project?

Are there any guidelines for educational use of this repository?

Thank you for creating and maintaining this project. It has been very helpful for learning and development.
